coredata.dc:description: Due to the higher susceptibility of alcohols and ketones to oxidation compared to alkanes, converting inert C-H bonds in alkanes into high value-added product KA oil with high selectivity is still a significant obstacle. In this study, flower-like microspheres of MoO3-x, featuring aligned nanosheets and oxygen vacancies, were combined with nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) to fabricate S-scheme heterojunctions of MoO3-x-NCDs for boosting the photocatalytic efficiency in oxidizing cyclohexane. Because of the oxygen vacancies-triggered localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) and the S-scheme charge transfer mechanism, there was a significant improvement in the absorption of visible light and an enhancement in the efficiency of charge separation. The MoO3-x-NCDs composites exhibited a notably improved catalytic performance in cyclohexane oxidation. The MoO3-x-NCDs composite exhibited remarkable selectivity of KA oil in catalyzing cyclohexane oxidation under light exposure, attributed to the substantial increase in cyclohexane adsorption capacity. The highest cyclohexane transformation rate of 9.98 % was achieved by MoO3-x-NCDs-2 composites with a MoO3-x/NCDs ratio of 50:1, outperforming bare MoO3-x by 1.81 times, while exhibiting a KA oil selectivity reaching 93.75 %. The mechanism of selective photocatalytic oxidation was explored using photoelectric examination, analysis of energy bands, and evaluation of active radicals. In this research, green photocatalysts were fabricated through the utilization of NCDs, enabling efficient conversion and selective oxidation of cyclohexane in gentle environments. This innovative approach holds great potential for advancing the field of photocatalysis.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the higher susceptibility of alcohols and ketones to oxidation compared to alkanes, converting inert C-H bonds in alkanes into high value-added product KA oil with high selectivity is still a significant obstacle. In this study, flower-like microspheres of MoO3-x, featuring aligned nanosheets and oxygen vacancies, were combined with nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) to fabricate S-scheme heterojunctions of MoO3-x-NCDs for boosting the photocatalytic efficiency in oxidizing cyclohexane. Because of the oxygen vacancies-triggered localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) and the S-scheme charge transfer mechanism, there was a significant improvement in the absorption of visible light and an enhancement in the efficiency of charge separation. The MoO3-x-NCDs composites exhibited a notably improved catalytic performance in cyclohexane oxidation. The MoO3-x-NCDs composite exhibited remarkable selectivity of KA oil in catalyzing cyclohexane oxidation under light exposure, attributed to the substantial increase in cyclohexane adsorption capacity. The highest cyclohexane transformation rate of 9.98 % was achieved by MoO3-x-NCDs-2 composites with a MoO3-x/NCDs ratio of 50:1, outperforming bare MoO3-x by 1.81 times, while exhibiting a KA oil selectivity reaching 93.75 %. The mechanism of selective photocatalytic oxidation was explored using photoelectric examination, analysis of energy bands, and evaluation of active radicals. In this research, green photocatalysts were fabricated through the utilization of NCDs, enabling efficient conversion and selective oxidation of cyclohexane in gentle environments. This innovative approach holds great potential for advancing the field of photocatalysis.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The current focus of chemical research lies in the advancement of eco-friendly, safer, and more efficient synthetic pathways. There is a growing industrial demand for valuable chemicals produced through the partial oxidation of saturated C-H bonds, making catalytic oxidation of saturated alkanes a key area of interest [1–3]. Particularly, the precise catalytic oxidation of cyclohexane has garnered significant attention in industrial and academic circles among various oxidation reactions. Due to the significance of its oxidation byproducts, namely cyclohexanol (CyOH) and cyclohexanone (Cy=O), referred to collectively as KA oil, they play a crucial role in the synthesis of adipic acid and ε-caprolactam [4,5], which are utilized as raw materials for nylon 6 and nylon 6,6 fibers. Nevertheless, the challenges encountered in the catalytic oxidation of cyclohexane lie in the activation of the unreactive C(sp3)-H bond and the management of KA's selectivity. Given the remarkable stability of the C(sp3)-H bond, it typically necessitates activation in harsh operating conditions [6,7]. Due to its susceptibility to oxidation compared to cyclohexane, maintaining cyclohexane conversion below 5 % is essential to achieve a high selectivity for KA oil (approximately 80∼85 %), albeit at the cost of increased energy consumption and lower conversion rates [8,9]. Some research has demonstrated improved efficiency in oxidizing cyclohexane catalytically by employing initiators like hydrogen peroxide and tert‑butyl peroxide [10,11]. Nevertheless, the use of initiators comes with elevated expenses and lacks industrial feasibility. Hence, it is crucial to create a gentle catalytic method that efficiently oxidizes cyclohexane at room temperature using air.

In the past few years, there has been a significant focus on the exploration of photocatalytic processes for both environmental remediation and the synthesis of organic compounds [12–14]. When light activates photocatalysts, it generates electrons and holes that initiate the activation of adsorbed substances, leading to the formation of free radicals which facilitate the production of alcohols, ketones, and various other compounds [15,16]. Semiconductor nanomaterials such as TiO2 [17], WO3 [18], bismuth based catalyst [19], and other catalysts have been reported for the photocatalytic oxidation of cyclohexane. However, their powerful oxidative properties and high affinity for KA oil led to a sustained surface oxidation process, ultimately resulting in only 40–70 % selectivity of KA oil in the photocatalytic oxidation process [17–19]. Hence, it is essential to develop innovative photocatalysts with limited redox properties and higher affinity for cyclohexane compared to KA oil to enhance the selectivity for KA oil.

MoO3-x possesses varying oxidation levels, outstanding light capture and heat energy conversion abilities, as well as being cost-effective [20]. The dimensions and structural variations, including nanorods, nanowires, and nanoparticles, can be tailored using a range of synthesis techniques to modulate the band-gap and surface affinity, thereby elevating the catalytic efficiency of MoO3-x [21]. Nevertheless, the intensified interactions among catalysts may inadvertently promote their aggregation. In comparison with two-dimensional structures, three-dimensional hierarchical structures exhibit superior capabilities in preventing aggregation and offering a significantly enlarged specific surface area along with active sites on the surface, which can effectively promote the diffusion of reactants [22,23]. Nevertheless, there remains a lack of documented methods for synthesizing MoO3-x featuring a flower-like microsphere structure. Many studies have shown that, faced with rapid charge recombination, creating the S-scheme heterojunction can effectively enhance the charge separation, maintain its strong redox capacity and greatly improve photocatalytic efficiency [24]. Nitrogen doped Carbon dots (NCDs) with dimensions less than 10 nm, involving electron-rich nitrogen atoms and terminal amino functional groups, are known for their affordability, adjustable energy levels, broad light absorption spectrum, and efficient charge transfer properties, markedly boosted catalytic performance [25,26]. It is strongly favored to combine flower-like microspheres of MoO3-x with NCDs to enhance carrier separation, achieve flexible reaction absorption, and greatly improve the photocatalytic performance in the cyclohexane oxidation process [27,28].

Thus, in this research, flower-like microspheres of MoO3-x with abundant oxygen gaps were produced via a solvothermal method, and nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles (NCDs) were affixed onto the surface. The structure, crystal phase, light absorption, and elemental chemical properties of the photocatalysts were scrutinized using a variety of methods. The aim was to evaluate the catalytic oxidation efficiency of MoO3-x-NCDs composites on cyclohexane under photocatalytic conditions. A mechanistic explanation for the photocatalytic oxidation by MoO3-x-NCDs composites was proposed based on the capture of active species, analysis of electron spin resonance signals, and energy band structure. It was observed that semiconductors modified with nitrogen-doped carbon dots have the potential to catalyze the oxidation of cyclohexane selectively, serving as an eco-friendly and gentle photocatalyst.

Ammonium molybdate (99 % purity) and anhydrous methanol were sourced from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory, while polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, with a molecular weight of 58,000), urea (99 % purity), citric acid (99 % purity) and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO, 97 %) were procured from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Nitric acid (65 %), absolute ethanol, hydrogen peroxide (30 %), and acetone were acquired from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory, with deionized water being generated in-house.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of MoO3-x flower-like microspheres and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Flower-like microspheres of MoO3-x were produced using a straightforward hydrothermal process. Initially, 500 mg of ammonium molybdate was dissolved in 5 mL of de-ionized water, followed by the addition of 1 mL of concentrated nitric acid to form a yellowish molybdate precursor. The resulting precursor underwent purification using a mixture of water and ethanol. Subsequently, alongside 6 mL of 30 % H2O2 solution and 500 mg of PVP, the homogeneous dispersion of the precursor in 20 mL of absolute ethanol was achieved. After subjecting the mixture to 30 min of ultrasonication, it was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon autoclave. The autoclave was then heated to 160 °C and maintained for 20 h before naturally cooling to room temperature. To finalize the process, the suspension was centrifuged at 9500 rpm for 5 min, and the resulting precipitate was washed twice with acetone, yielding a final product in the form of dark blue powder.
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using citric acid and urea, following the same procedure as our previous report [29].

To combine NCDs with MoO3-x sheet spheres, 100 mg of MoO3-x microspheres were carefully dispersed in 50 mL of anhydrous ethanol by ultrasonication for 10 min. Subsequently, NCDs aqueous solutions of 2 mg•mL-1 were introduced in quantities of 0.5, 1, 2.5, and 5 mL, and the mixture was sonicated for 20 min. Following this, the amalgamation underwent magnetic stirring for a period of 24 h. The resulting sediments were obtained through centrifugation of the blends at 9500 rpm for 5 min and labeled as MoO3-x-NCDs-X (X = 1, 2, 5, and 10). Following vacuum drying, the product was preserved in a desiccator.

Utilizing transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi HT7700, Japan), high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Thermo Fisher Talos F200S, Czech Republic) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi SU8220, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV in conjunction with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), we conducted an examination of the structure and elemental composition of the samples. The crystallographic structure of the catalysts was examined through X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis utilizing Cu-Kα radiation (Bruker D8ADVANCE, Germany). Experimental data from X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) and Ultraviolet Photoelectron Spectroscopy (UPS) were gathered with the utilization of a spectrometer (Thermo-Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi, England). The reference point for all binding energies was established at the C 1s peak, specifically at 284.8 eV. Samples, consisting of compressed thin slices of dried potassium bromide powder, were meticulously prepared for analysis. The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra of these samples were then recorded employing a FTIR spectrometer (Thermo-Fisher Nicolet 6700, US). Furthermore, the UV–vis spectra were captured using a UV-visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus, China), with BaSO4 serving as the reflectance standard. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were measured with a photoluminescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Instruments Incorporated, US). We captured transient photocurrent responses, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS), and Mott Schottky curves using an electrochemical workstation (Autolab PGSTA T302N, Metrohm), a standard three-electrode electrolytic cell and a 300 W Xenon lamp (Zhongjiao Jinyuan Co. Ltd.). The transient photocurrent was measured with an applied bias of 0.2 V. The EIS measurements were carried out in the frequency range 105–0.1 Hz at zero bias potential under open-circuit reaction conditions. The MS measurements were acquired in the dark at a frequency of 600 and 800 Hz with a potential of 50 mV. 5 μL of catalyst dispersion with the concentration of 10 g/L was uniformly applied to the glassy carbon electrode, followed by a covering of 5 μL Nafion solution as the working electrode. The electrode setup included a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference and Pt as the counter electrode, with a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution serving as the electrolyte. To determine the active radicals in the system, the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, Bruker EMX plus-10/12, Germany) spectrometer was employed in conjunction with 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin capture agent. 10 mg of the samples underwent ultrasonic dispersion in 2 mL of deionized water for the assessment of hydroxyl radicals, and in 2 mL of anhydrous methanol for analyzing superoxide radicals. Subsequently, 5 μL of DMPO was added, and the solution was exposed to a 300 W Xenon lamp for 5 min for EPR detection.

The photochemical process took place using a versatile photochemical reactor from CEAULIGHT (Zhongjiao Jinyuan Co. Ltd.), which included a quartz tube (Φ20 × 180 mm), a 500 W Hg lamp (emission spectrum of 365 nm), and a recirculating cooling trap. 30 mg of catalyst was mixed thoroughly with 5 mL of acetone and 1 mL of cyclohexane using ultrasonic dispersion, and then the reaction solution was transferred to a quartz tube. The mixture was agitated at room temperature for 30 min until a uniform mixture was achieved, following which the light was turned on. The reaction proceeded for 12 h with samples taken every 3 h. Following the centrifugation process, the catalysts were isolated. The liquid products resulting from the oxidation of cyclohexane were then mixed with the internal standard before undergoing analysis using GC-7920A equipment from Zhongjiao Jinyuan Co. Ltd. The collected GC data was interpolated onto the standard curve. By adding triphenylphosphine to the blend, the conversion of cyclohexyl hydrogen peroxide (CHHP) to cyclohexanol and cyclohexanone was accomplished. The concentration of CHHP was determined by monitoring the variations in cyclohexanol and cyclohexanone levels. Subsequently, the gaseous by-product was captured and analyzed using GC-9800 instrument from KE CHUANG. The performance outcomes of KA oil were then evaluated. The conversion of cyclohexane and the selectivity of the oxygenated products were calculated based on the following equations:

Additionally, catalytic experiments for oxidizing cyclohexanol were conducted using the previously mentioned reaction setup. Prior to the reaction, 30 mg of the catalyst was dispersed in 5 mL of acetone and 1 mL of cyclohexanol through ultrasonic means. Subsequently, this mixture was added to the quartz tube to initiate the reaction. The conversion of cyclohexanol and the selectivity of cyclohexanone were determined using the equations below:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structures and characterization of MoO3-x-NCDS catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic efficiency of small-sized nanomaterials is significantly compromised by their tendency to aggregate and the presence of excessive surfactants. On the contrary, microspheres made up of numerous small nano units not only maintain the small size and large specific surface area of nanomaterials but also prevent aggregation, demonstrating promising applications in catalysis. In our prior research, we prepared a series of MoO3-x nanorods with polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) acting as ligands. It was observed that acidic environments promoted the aggregation of MoO3-x nanoparticles [30,31]. Drawn from this issue, we combined a suitable volume of hydrogen peroxide with PVP, which led to the formation of MoO3-x nanosheet microspheres. Across reaction temperatures ranging from 120 to 180 °C, all MoOx samples maintained a spherical shape, demonstrating the essential role played by PVP and H2O2 in determining their morphology (Fig. S1). The formation of uniform microspheres consisting of thin layers of nanosheets was achieved at 160 °C (Fig. 1 a, b). As the reduction effect of H2O2, the microspheres appeared dark blue in color, attributed to the generation of oxygen vacancies (OVs) and Mo5+ [32]. Moreover, as the hydrothermal temperature reached 180 °C, the MoO3-x microspheres turned completely dark, indicating increased production of MoO2, as evidenced by the XRD patterns in Fig. S2. Insufficient H2O2 led to the formation of microspheres measuring 1–2 μm with a sleek exterior (Fig. S3), further validating the critical role of H2O2 in fabricating MoO3-x nanosheet microspheres.

Analysis of the TEM images of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) in Fig. 1c reveals a uniform distribution of nanoparticles in sizes ranging from 1.9 to 5 nm, with an average size of approximately 3.50 nm. Furthermore, the HR-TEM image in Fig. 1d shows a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm for the NCDs, corresponding to the (1 0 0) crystallographic plane of graphitic carbon [33,34]. Due to the opposite zeta potentials of MoO3-x and NCDs (2.08 and −1.01 mV, respectively, Fig. S4), they could be strongly drawn together by electrostatic forces to generate the MoO3-x-NCDs composite. TEM imaging was used to examine the structures of MoO3-x-NCDs composites, as depicted in Fig. 1e–h, revealing the uniform distribution of carbon dots on the surface of MoO3-x microspheres. It was significant that the NCDs combination does not visibly change the morphology of the MoO3-x microsphere. The EDS analysis revealed a uniform distribution of both molybdenum (Mo), oxygen (O), carbon (C) and nitrogen (N) elements within the MoO3-x-NCDs composite spheres, as shown in Fig. 1i–m. Consequently, it is evident that successful complexation between MoO3-x and NCDs has been achieved, with NCDs evenly distributed on the MoO3-x surface. These composites were designed to increase the specific surface area for greater exposure of active sites, facilitating efficient interfacial charge transfer due to the close interaction between NCDs and MoO3-x.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique was used to analyze the crystalline structures of MoO3-x and MoO3-x-NCDs complexes with varying mass ratios, as illustrated in Fig. 2 a. The MoO3-x microspheres exhibited diffraction peaks at 10.81°, 25.56°, 34.17°, and 47.75°, corresponding to the crystallographic facets of Mo8O23 (JCPDS No. 05-0339), specifically (2 0 0), (0 1 2), (2 1 3), and (1 2 2). This led to the identification of the molybdenum oxide as MoO3-x. In contrast, the XRD patterns of MoO3-x-NCDs exhibited consistent intensity and peak positions, with no apparent alterations compared to MoO3-x. Despite the presence of NCDs, their limited loading and low diffraction intensity resulted in the absence of discernible distinct peaks in the XRD pattern of MoO3-x-NCDs [35]. The functional groups of the synthesized samples were identified using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). In Fig. 2b, the FT-IR spectra of the MoO3-x-NCDs composite closely resembled the typical vibrational modes of MoO3-x, indicating that the incorporation of NCDs did not change the molecular structure of MoO3-x. The original MoO3-x displays clear peak points at 633 and 981 cm-1, indicating the distinct stretching vibrations of O-Mo-O and Mo=O [41]. The shift to the left of the Mo=O vibration peak to 898 cm-1 in the MoO3-x-NCDs composite suggests a robust interaction between NCDs and MoO3-x. The unique spectral signal seen in the 1300–1500 cm-1 range of MoO3-x-NCDs composite, distinct from MoO3-x, corresponds to the stretching vibration of CN bonds, highlighting the compositional difference [36,37]. The prominent peaks at 1660 and 1672 cm-1 in MoO3-x and MoO3-x-NCDs composites are attributed to the vibrational stretching of the C=O bond originating from the residual PVP ligands on the surface of MoO3-x and the NCDs in the MoO3-x-NCDs composites. Meanwhile, distinctive peaks at 1623 and 2950 cm-1 in the MoO3-x-NCDs composite signify the presence of C=N and C-H stretching vibrations [36,38], respectively. These vibrations correlate with the broader N-H and O-H bands between 3100 and 3500 cm-1 [39,40], providing clear evidence of amine bonds within the NCDs. Overall, the unique spectral features of the MoO3-x-NCDs composite confirm the successful synthesis of this composite material.

The UV-visible spectra in Fig. 2c illustrate the light capture characteristics of MoO3-x and the MoO3-x-NCDs composites with varying NCD concentrations. In comparison to MoO3-x microspheres, the MoO3-x-NCDs combinations exhibit a subtle improvement in light capture, mainly attributed to NCDs' light absorption properties at 200–300 and 330 nm wavelengths (Fig. S5), deriving from the π-π* leaps of the C=C structure and an amino group's n-π* transitions [26,41]. The interband absorption threshold of MoO3-x is around 410 nm, but with the introduction of NCDs, the threshold shifts towards longer wavelengths at 430 nm for MoO3-x-NCDs-10 sample. All the composite samples of MoO3-x-NCDs demonstrated significant light absorption properties within the visible and near-infrared ranges, attributed to oxygen vacancies that activate the Localized Surface Plasmon Resonance (LSPR) effect [25,48,49]. Interestingly, these composite samples exhibited no noticeable deviations when compared to the original MoO3-x samples. The existence of oxygen vacancies was further confirmed through electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy. In Fig. 2d, a clearly identifiable Lorentz-type signal with g = 1.92 was observed, indicating the presence of free unpaired electrons with paramagnetic properties, which were released from oxygen vacancies bound to Mo5+ ions [50,51].

To delve deeper into the elemental composition, chemical bonding, and oxidation states, XPS analysis was conducted. The high-resolution spectra reveal two main components corresponding to Mo6+ and Mo5+ oxidation states in the Mo 3d core-level spectra in Fig. 3 a. The peaks observed at 232.6 and 235.5 eV in the spectrum represent the spin states of Mo 3d 5/2 and Mo 3d 3/2 under the Mo6+ oxidation states. Conversely, the binding energies of Mo5+ at 231.3 and 234.3 eV are designated for the 3d 5/2 and 3d 3/2 states [42]. Interestingly, the binding energy of Mo 3d appears slightly lower in MoO3-x-NCDs-2 than in MoO3-x, implying a notable interaction between MoO3-x and NCDs [43]. By analyzing the integrated area of the Mo 3d peak, the ratios of Mo5+ to Mo6+ in MoO3-x and MoO3-x-NCDs-2 samples were 9:10 and 1:3 and, respectively, suggesting the partial depletion of surface OVs and Mo5+ on MoO3-x when combined with NCDs [44]. In Fig. 3b, the O 1s peak of MoO3-x can be resolved into three distinct peaks at 530.2, 530.9, and 532.4 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen, oxygen vacancies, and oxygen adsorbed on the surface, respectively [45,46]. When combined with NCDs, the peaks shift to 530.2, 531.7, and 533.2 eV, indicating the presence of oxygen within lattice oxygen, C=O bonds, and C=O bonds from NCDs. The data presented in Fig. 3c and d provide strong evidence of the presence of C and N within the MoO3-x-NCDs-2 samples. In the C 1s spectra, four distinct peaks at 288.7, 287.5, 286.1, and 284.8 eV represent different chemical bonds: O-C=O/N-C=O, C=N, C-N, and C-C/C=C [47,48]. Moreover, the N 1s spectrum of the MoO3-x-NCDs-2 sample shows three key peaks at 397.7, 399.6, and 401.5 eV, indicating N-(C)3 bonding, sp2-bonded N (C-N=C), and N-H bonding from tertiary nitrogen groups. The XPS analysis confirms the successful creation of MoO3-x-NCDs hybrids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic performance in the cyclohexane oxidation
ce:section.ce:para: The changes in photocatalytic cyclohexane oxidation conversion and KA oil selectivity over time are illustrated in Figs. 4 a–c and S6. Following a 12 h photocatalytic reaction, the cyclohexane conversion rates for MoO3-x, MoO3-x-NCDs-1, MoO3-x-NCDs-2, MoO3-x-NCDs-5, and MoO3-x-NCDs-10 stood at 5.51 %, 7.22 %, 9.98 %, 8.29 %, and 7.57 % respectively. The selectivity of pristine MoO3-x was a mere 84 %. However, the MoO3-x-NCDs composite catalysts exhibited KA oil selectivity exceeding 90 %. As the photocatalytic reaction time increases, the conversion is gradually enhanced for all catalysts. There was a slight 5.3 % decrease in the selectivity of KA oil for original MoO3-x. In contrast, the selectivity of KA oil for MoO3-x-NCDs composites experienced only a marginal 1.5 % decrease, highlighting the substantial enhancement in KA oil selectivity due to the NCDs combination. Furthermore, as the reaction time increases, the selectivity of cyclohexanol decreases gradually, whereas the selectivity of cyclohexanone shows a slight increase, implying the gradual oxidation into cyclohexanone during the photocatalytic process. Among these samples, MoO3-x-NCDs-2 exhibited the most effective catalytic efficiency after a 12 h reaction, showing a cyclohexane conversion rate of 9.98 % and a KA oil selectivity of 93.75 %. The calculated maximum rate for MoO3-x-NCDs-2 reached 3129 μmol·h-1·g-1, showcasing a remarkable 1.81-fold enhancement from the initial performance of MoO3-x (Fig. S7).

To examine the photocatalytic durability of MoO3-x-NCDs-2 catalysts in the oxidation of cyclohexane, a series of reusability experiments were conducted over five cycles (Fig. 4d). Surprisingly, the decrease in performance of the MoO3-x-NCDs-2 catalysts was marginal, peaking at only 0.76 %. Analysis of TEM images and XRD patterns (Fig. S8) revealed that the crystal structure and morphology of the MoO3-x-NCDs-2 catalysts remained unchanged before and after the photocatalytic reaction, underscoring the dependable catalytic stability of MoO3-x-NCDs-2. Furthermore, a gradual increase in the K/A ratio was observed as the reaction progressed.

Due to the gradual oxidation of cyclohexanol to cyclohexanone in the process of photocatalytic oxidation, we conducted a selective photocatalytic oxidation of cyclohexanol to cyclohexanone using various catalysts. Among them, the MoO3-x-NCDs-2 sample exhibited superior performance in catalyzing the oxidation of cyclohexanol compared to MoO3-x (Fig. S9). After a 12 h reaction, the conversion rate of cyclohexanol using MoO3-x-NCDs-2 reached 18.05 %, resulting in a cyclohexanone selectivity of 89.54 % with a cyclohexanone production rate of 4606 μmol·h-1·g-1.

To delve into the factors behind the enhanced selectivity of KA oil facilitated by composite catalysts, trials involving the adsorption of cyclohexane, cyclohexanol, and cyclohexanone were carried out. Within these experiments, 30 mg of MoO3-x and MoO3-x-NCDs-2 samples was added to a 6 mL solution of acetone mixed with 1 μmol·L-1 each of cyclohexane, cyclohexanol, and cyclohexanone, respectively. After an hour of stirring at ambient temperature, the mixture underwent analysis through gas chromatography (Fig. 4e-f). The findings reveal that MoO3-x-NCDs-2 composite catalyst stands out for its ability to attract cyclohexane effectively at 30 °C compared to cyclohexanol and cyclohexanone during the joint adsorption process. Particularly noteworthy is the remarkable enhancement in cyclohexane adsorption achieved by the MoO3-x-NCDs-2 composite, increasing from 45 % to 73 %, outperforming the original MoO3-x material. The MoO3-x-NCDs-2 composite effectively adsorbed cyclohexane at 30 °C, while showing reduced adsorption on KA oil during co-adsorption. This phenomenon is linked to the decreased surface polarity and increased active sites for cyclohexane adsorption after the introduction of NCDs, enhancing selectivity and preventing excessive oxidation. In contrast to the studies summarized in Table 1 , the MoO3-x-NCDs-2 composites exhibit superior photocatalytic performance in the oxidation of cyclohexane with simultaneously high conversion rate and high selectivity of KA oil. The findings validate its prowess as a stellar catalyst in the targeted oxidation of cyclohexane for KA oil production.

The efficiency of separating and transferring photo-induced charge carriers plays a crucial role in photocatalytic oxidation–reduction reactions. According to studies using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, it was revealed that each sample displayed distinct emission peaks at 396 nm (Fig. 5 a). Among them, the MoO3-x-NCDs-2 sample demonstrated the most significant PL quenching, indicating that the creation of heterojunctions enhances charge transfer and inhibits the recombination of electron-hole pairs generated by light in MoO3-x and NCDs. This phenomenon aligns with their remarkable catalytic performance. To explore the transient photocurrent response of MoO3-x and MoO3-x-NCDs composite, intermittent visible light exposure is recommended. In general, the faster the recombination rate of photoexcited charges, the lower the instantaneous photocurrent density (Fig. 5b). Higher photocurrent responses reflect efficient charge separation in semiconductors under light exposure. The MoO3-x-NCDs-2 sample, showing the highest photocurrent density, demonstrates the most effective charge separation, suggesting that the inclusion of NCDs can significantly enhance the transfer efficiency of photo-induced carriers in MoO3-x, aligning with its optimum catalytic performance. Furthermore, the impedance of charge transfer in the sample was analyzed using EIS Nyquist. In Fig. 5c, it was observed that the MoO3-x-NCDs-2 sample exhibited the most compact arch shape, indicating the lowest resistance of MoO3-x-NCDs-2. These findings collectively suggest that the incorporation of appropriate amounts of NCDs improves the efficiency of charge separation and transfer in MoO3-x.

The band structures of MoO3-x and NCDs were analyzed through a combination of ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), Mott-Schottky curves, and ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). By utilizing the Kubelka-Munk function equation, the band gap energies (Eg) were determined to be 2.85 and 3.38 eV for MoO3-x and NCDs (Fig. 6 a and b). To explore the essential operational characteristics of MoO3-x and NCDs, we conducted Mott-Schottky analysis at frequencies of 600 and 800 Hz, as illustrated in Fig. 6c and d. The experimental findings indicate that the flat band potentials for the MoO3-x and NCDs electrodes, in comparison with the Ag/AgCl reference, are recorded as -0.39 and -0.23 eV, respectively. Hence, the detected potentials are converted into the potential of the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) (ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.197), and the calculated conduction band energy (ECB) for MoO3-x and NCD are at -0.19 and -0.03 eV, respectively [53]. Based on the band gap energy and ECB data, the valence band levels (EVB) of MoO3-x and NCDs were found to be located at 2.66 and 3.35 eV, respectively, relative to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). Moreover, the band positions of MoO3-x-NCDs were determined using ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) with a He I excitation source (hv=21.22 eV) in Fig. 6e and f. The secondary electron cutoffs (Ecutoff) for MoO3-x and NCDs were calculated to be 16.85 and 16.63 eV, respectively. To standardize the test, the EF value was adjusted to 0 eV by linking the samples to Ag during the test. The vacuum work functions (WF) for MoO3-x and NCD were determined as 4.37 and 4.59 eV, respectively, calculated by the formula WF = hv + EFermi - Ecutoff (where hν equals 21.22 eV). By applying the equation Evacum = -ERHE - 4.44 eV, the Fermi energy levels of MoO3-x and NCDs in relation to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) can be simplified to −0.07 and 0.15 eV [54].

By analyzing the energy band structure and Fermi energy levels of the samples, we investigated the interfacial charge transfer behavior of MoO3-x-NCDs composites. As shown in Fig. 7 a–c, electrons flow from the higher Fermi energy levels of MoO3-x to the lower NCDs until the Fermi energy levels of the two semiconductors reach equilibrium, and thus the positively charged MoO3-x and the negatively charged NCDs will form an internal electric field at the interface. The electrons in MoO3-x are repulsed by the internal electric field, the potential energy increases and the energy band bends upwards. On the contrary, NCDs energy bands bend downward. Under light irradiation, MoO3-x and NCDs are excited simultaneously and the photogenerated electrons jump from VB to CB. At the same time, the photogenerated electrons on the CB of the NCDs are quenched with holes on the VB of MoO3-x via interfacial transfer due to the driving of the internal electric field. Based on the above analysis, this S-scheme electron transfer mechanism not only preserves the photogenerated electrons in the CB of MoO3-x with stronger reducing ability and the holes in the VB of NCDs with strong oxidizing ability, but also promotes the photogenerated carrier separation of MoO3-x-NCDs composites [24,55,56]. Moreover, the TEM images in Fig. S10 reveal the intricate arrangement of the composite material consisting of MoO3-x-NCDs and silver. Remarkably, the silver ions are transformed into silver nanoparticles on the surface of MoO3-x microspheres through the interaction with electrons from the conduction band of MoO3-x, further demonstrating the S-scheme electron transfer mechanism of MoO3-x-NCDs.

To explore the role and reaction mechanism of active free radicals in the photocatalytic process, we employed experiments capturing active species and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) detection techniques. p-BQ, IPA, and Methanol were used as trappers for ∙O2 - radicals, ∙OH radicals, and h + [52]. The outcomes of the experiments are depicted in Fig. 7d. After introducing p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and methanol, the cyclohexane conversion rate dropped significantly by 49.1 %, 39.6 % and 24.2 % respectively, indicating the crucial roles played by all the active centers in catalyzing reactions under light exposure. Notably, the presence of p-BQ and IPA led to a more pronounced decline in the catalytic activity of the MoO3-x-NCDs-2 sample. This suggests that the impact of ∙O2 - radicals and ∙OH radicals on cyclohexane oxidation outweighs that of h +. Results from active substance analysis confirm that ∙O2 - radicals and ∙OH radicals are the primary drivers of the photocatalytic oxidation of cyclohexane.

The catalyst-induced formation of ∙O2 - and ∙OH radicals was investigated through EPR analysis, employing 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the collector for ∙OH and ∙O2 - radicals [52,53]. In the aqueous medium, the presence of DMPO-∙OH spin adducts manifested by four distinct peaks in a 1:2:2:1 ratio (Fig. 7e). Notably, the peak intensity of MoO3-x-NCDs-2 surpassed that of MoO3-x due to the superior charge separation efficiency achieved upon NCDs incorporation, resulting in heightened generation of ∙OH radicals during the photocatalytic process. Based on the data presented in Fig. 7f, the MoO3-x-NCDs-2 sample demonstrates a stronger ESR signal for ∙O2 - in comparison to the pristine MoO3-x, indicating a higher generation of superoxide radicals under identical conditions. Interestingly, under dark conditions, no traces of DMPO-·OH and DMPO-·O2 - adducts were detected in Fig. 7e, f. Notably, EPR analysis suggests that the MoO3-x-NCDs heterojunction significantly boosts the levels of ∙O2 - and ∙OH free radicals.

Following the characterization and analysis above, a potential process for the photocatalytic oxidation of cyclohexane using MoO3-x-NCDs composites is suggested in Fig. 8 . Under mercury lamp light, the S-scheme heterojunction effectively separates the holes within NCDs and the electrons within MoO3-x. The holes in NCDs facilitate the oxidation of cyclohexane, producing the cyclohexyl radical (C6H11∙) which further reacts with O2 to generate cyclohexyl peroxide radical (C6H11OO∙). Subsequently, the coupling reaction of the two C6H11OO∙ molecules produce cyclohexanol and cyclohexanone. Meanwhile, the electrons gathered at the CB of MoO3-x reduced O2 to form ∙O2 -, which participated in producing H2O2. Then, H2O2 combined with C6H11∙ to create a substance known as cyclohexyl peroxide (C6H11OOH). Later, the breakdown of H2O2 and C6H11OOH resulted in the formation of ∙OH and C6H11O∙, separately. Moreover, regeneration of C6H11∙ is possible through the process of removing H from cyclohexane by ∙OH and C6H11O∙, which resulted in the production of cyclohexanol. The generated cyclohexanol is susceptible to be further oxidized into cyclohexanone by holes at the surface, leading to a gradual rise in cyclohexanone [8,57]. Therefore, NCDs effectively have a crucial impact on regulating interface electron transfer in MoO3-x-NCDs composite. The distinctive S-scheme heterojunction effectively suppressed the recombination of photo-generated electron and hole pairs, leading to a remarkable increase in the levels of active ∙O2 - and ∙OH radicals. Consequently, a superior photocatalytic performance with elevated conversion rate and enhanced selectivity simultaneously was attained when conducting cyclohexane oxidation.

To summarize, MoO3-x nanosheet microspheres with oxygen vacancies were synthesized via a hydrothermal approach involving PVP and H2O2, and then combined with NCDs to form MoO3-x-NCDs composite through electrostatic attraction. The uniform MoO3-x microspheres, containing oxygen vacancies, exhibit a 1–2 µm diameter and consist of overlapping nanosheets. NCDs of around 3.5 nm are uniformly adhered to the surface of MoO3-x microspheres without altering its dimensions and crystal structure in MoO3-x-NCDs compound. All the MoO3-x-NCDs composites demonstrate a significant enhancement in the conversion rate of cyclohexane, along with a notably higher KA oil selectivity compared to the pristine MoO3-x. The MoO3-x-NCDs-2 composite with a weight ratio of MoO3-x/NCDs at 50:1 demonstrates the most effective photocatalytic performance, achieving a 9.98 % conversion rate of cyclohexane and a 93.75 % selectivity after 12 h of reaction. Notably, the KA oil production rate peaks at 3129 μmol∙h-1∙g-1, marking a substantial 1.81-fold increase compared to MoO3-x alone. The significantly enhanced adsorption of cyclohexane by MoO3-x-NCDs relative to pristine MoO3-x microsphere contributes to the evidently increased KA oil selectivity. The unique S-scheme heterojunction in MoO3-x-NCDs composites efficiently suppresses the recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, markedly enhancing the levels of active ∙O2 - and ∙OH radicals, thereby significantly enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency for cyclohexane oxidation. The upcoming work will delve deeper into the impact of CB and VB energy levels of photocatalysts, as well as the influence of surface adsorption on the excessive oxidation of KA oil. This study offers guidance on achieving great selectivity at high conversion rates in photocatalytic selective oxidation under mild conditions.
